coredata.dc:title: Study on preparation of high yield lignin-based carbon dots by hydrothermal oxidation process and optical regulation
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Hanping
Zeng, Jiawen
Chen, Zhiqiang
Xia, Mingwei
Gao, Ying
Zhu, Danchen
Tang, Ziyue
Yang, Haiping
Chen, Yingquan
Wang, Xianhua
coredata.dc:description: To improve the yield and fluorescence characteristic of lignin-based carbon dots, a two-step process of hydrothermal and oxidation was proposed to achieve efficient preparation of intense fluorescent lignin-based carbon dots (LCDs) in this study. N, S co-doped LCDs (NS-LCDs) and hydrothermal char were prepared by using mild sulfanilic acid as additive, then hydrothermal char as carbon source was oxidized and exfoliated to prepare high-yield oxidized LCDs (O-LCDs) via using NaOH and H2O2. It was found that the yields of NS-LCDs initially increased and then decreased with hydrothermal temperature increasing, reaching the maximum value of 5.71 % at 200 °C. And more than 50 % of the hydrothermal char was converted to O-LCDs by oxidative stripping, which resulted in the total yield of LCDs reached to 17.13 % at 200 °C. Meanwhile, the fluorescence quantum yield of NS-LCDs showed a similar trend, obtained a maximum value of 18.00 % at 200 °C, which was the same as content of C sp2 and C-N in XPS results. And the average particle size of NS-LCDs was less than O-LCDs, the average particle size 180-NS-LCDs was 3.61 nm, while that of 180-O-LCDs was increased to 3.92 nm. In addition, when the temperature was higher than 180 °C, the fluorescence lifetime of NS-LCDs was prolonged, which might be related to N and S doping, and 200-NS-LCDs showed good acid stability, temperature stability and photobleaching resistance. This study provides guidance for the application and preparation of high-quality LCDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Alkali lignin
Hydrothermal reaction
Oxidation
N/ S co-doped carbon dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The yields of LCDs and solid residue
Effect on chemical constitution of LCDs
Effect on optical properties of LCDs
Regulation mechanism of NS-LCDs and O-LCDs
Analysis of fluorescence stability of NS-LCDs

And the technical support from the Analytical and Testing Center at Huazhong University of Science & Technology ( http://atc.hust.edu.cn ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The preparation of LCDs by hydrothermal oxidation two-step process diagram.
Yields of NS-LCDs, O-LCDs and solid residue at different reaction temperatures.
TEM image of 180-NS-LCDs (a)(b); TEM images of 180-O-LCDs (c)(d); Particle size distribution of 180-NS-LCDs (e); Particle size distribution of 180-O-LCDs (f).
Functional group characterization of LCDs at different temperatures. FT-IR spectrum of NS-LCDs (a); FT-IR diagram of O-LCDs (b).

The XPS survey spectra of LCDs, NS-LCDs (a) O-LCDs(b); the high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s, O 1s, N 1s, and S 2p of LCDs, (c) C 1s of NS-LCDs;(d) O 1s of NS-LCDs;(e)N 1s of NS-LCDs;(f)S 2p of NS-LCDs; (g) C 1s of O-LCDs;(h) O 1s of O-LCDs;(i)N 1s of O-LCDs;(j)S 2p of O-LCDs.

UV–vis spectra and daylight/violet light photographs of LCDs solution,(a)160-NS-LCDs;(b)180-NS-LCDs;(c)200-NS-LCDs;(d)220-NS-LCDs;(e)160-O-LCDs;(f)180-O-LCDs;(g)200-O-LCDs;(h)220-O-LCDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Structure and fluorescence properties of O-LCDs and NS-LCDs.

Fluorescence pH stability test of 200-NS-LCDs: PL spectra (a), the relative integral fluorescence intensity (b); the fluorescence temperature stability test of 200-NS-LCDs: PL spectra (c), the relative integral fluorescence intensity(d).
The fluorescence kinetics steady-state test of 200-NS-LCDs.
The optimum excitation wavelength, optimum emission wavelength, and QY of NS-LCDs and O-LCDs.
Fitting parameters of fluorescence lifetime decay curves of NS-LCDs and O-LCDs.

head.ce:title: Study on preparation of high yield lignin-based carbon dots by hydrothermal oxidation process and optical regulation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Key Laboratory of Coal Combustion and Low Carbon Utilization, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, 1037 Luoyu Road, 430074, Wuhan, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Key Laboratory of Coal Combustion and Low Carbon Utilization
Huazhong University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National Key Laboratory of Coal Combustion and Low Carbon Utilization, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, 1037 Luoyu Road, 430074, Wuhan, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Energy Science and Engineering, Nanjing Tech University, Nanjing, 211816, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Energy Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Energy Science and Engineering, Nanjing Tech University, Nanjing, 211816, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To improve the yield and fluorescence characteristic of lignin-based carbon dots, a two-step process of hydrothermal and oxidation was proposed to achieve efficient preparation of intense fluorescent lignin-based carbon dots (LCDs) in this study. N, S co-doped LCDs (NS-LCDs) and hydrothermal char were prepared by using mild sulfanilic acid as additive, then hydrothermal char as carbon source was oxidized and exfoliated to prepare high-yield oxidized LCDs (O-LCDs) via using NaOH and H2O2. It was found that the yields of NS-LCDs initially increased and then decreased with hydrothermal temperature increasing, reaching the maximum value of 5.71 % at 200 °C. And more than 50 % of the hydrothermal char was converted to O-LCDs by oxidative stripping, which resulted in the total yield of LCDs reached to 17.13 % at 200 °C. Meanwhile, the fluorescence quantum yield of NS-LCDs showed a similar trend, obtained a maximum value of 18.00 % at 200 °C, which was the same as content of C sp2 and C-N in XPS results. And the average particle size of NS-LCDs was less than O-LCDs, the average particle size 180-NS-LCDs was 3.61 nm, while that of 180-O-LCDs was increased to 3.92 nm. In addition, when the temperature was higher than 180 °C, the fluorescence lifetime of NS-LCDs was prolonged, which might be related to N and S doping, and 200-NS-LCDs showed good acid stability, temperature stability and photobleaching resistance. This study provides guidance for the application and preparation of high-quality LCDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), which are carbon nanomaterials with dimensions less than 10 nm in three dimensions, have wide application prospects in the fields of fluorescence anti-counterfeiting, biological imaging, optoelectronic devices, and photocatalysis with the advantages of excellent optical properties, water solubility, biocompatibility [1]. And hydrothermal is a promising method to prepare CDs [2,3]. Spessato et al. [4] obtained high yield CDs by hydrothermal treat from acerola bagasse, and employed as dopants to enhance the photocatalytic efficiency of Nb2O5. Raji et al. [5] synthesized highly durable fluorescent nitrogen-doped CDs from utilizing Phyllanthus acidus and aqueous ammonia as the carbon and nitrogen sources via one-step hydrothermal method. Besides, lignin is the second richest organic substance with a natural aromatic ring structure, which serves as a potential precursor for carbon materials [6,7]. Nawaz et al. [8] proposed that lignin-based carbon dots (LCDs) prepared by hydrothermal method exhibited the advantages of small size, high quantum yield, biocompatibility, non-toxicity, photostability and substantial tunable emission. Yang et al. [9] prepared N, Mg co-doped LCDs by hydrothermal method using magnesium hydroxide and ethylenediamine as additives. Zhu et al. [10] prepared LCDs with blue to yellow-green fluorescence expression using four organic acids as additives and applied them to anti-counterfeit printing.

However, in the actual hydrothermal process, excessive carbonization, functional group loss and aggregation of LCDs are unavoidable, which can lead to the decrease of water solubility, fluorescence intensity and stability of LCDs, thus limiting its application. By adjusting the size and graphitization degree of LCDs [11], or modifying by doping and passivation [12], the optical properties of LCDS can be effectively regulated and its application range can be further expanded. Atom doping is an efficient method to change the electronic structure of LCDs [13]. And many studies have proved that heteroatom doping can increase the quantum yield of LCDs [14]. Yuan et al. [15] successfully prepared N-self-doped LCDs by one-pot hydrothermal method, and its quantum yield reached 9.2 %. Compared with the same raw material LCDs, the doped LCDs exhibited better fluorescence characteristics. Liao et al. [16] found that N exists mainly in the form of pyridine N, pyrrole N and graphite-like N in the carbon nuclei of LCDs, while nitrogen exists mainly in the form of amide on the surface of LCDs. Yang et al. [17] used lignin as raw material and concentrated sulfuric acid as acid hydrolysis and dopant to prepare S-doped LCDs by hydrothermal method, and the quantum yield of LCDs was increased from 3 % to 13.5 %. Besides, compared with single heteroatom doping, the co-doping of multiple heteroatoms might produce a complex synergistic effect, which has a more complex effect on the fluorescence characteristics of LCDs. Lin et al. [18] used aminobenzene sulfonic acid as dopant to prepare N and S co-doped LCDs, which increased the fluorescence QY from 1.66 % to 45.05 %. Yang et al. [9] used lignin as carbon source and prepared N and Mg co-doped LCDs by hydrothermal synthesis with ethylenediamine and magnesium hydroxide as dopants, which causes the emission wavelength to redshift in comparison with that of N-doped LCDs. And Gu et al. [19] synthesized N and B co-doped LCDs, which has three different emission centers of purple, blue and green. In addition, the presence of heteroatoms in LCDs also has a significant impact on its optical properties [20]. However, the influence mechanism and regulation mechanism of the presence of nitrogen and sulfur in LCDs on the optical properties of LCDs are still unclear and need further study.

On the other hand, low yield is a common problem in CDs preparation. Many researchers are committed to improving the yield of CDs by optimizing the process or preparation methods [21]. Zhu et al. [22] carried out acid hydrolysis of lignin with relatively mild o-aminobenzoic sulfonic acid, and prepared LCDs with yields of 30.2 % and 15.6 % by hydrothermal treatment of the collected filtrate and filter residue, respectively. Wang et al. [23] first pyrolyzed rice husks to produce char products, and then used char as raw material to prepare CDs with 25 % mass yield through HNO3 oxidation. Zhao et al. [24] firstly performed hydrothermal reaction on the three components of biomass respectively to produce hydrothermal char, and then oxidize the hydrothermal char with NaOH and O2 to prepare CDs with yield of 42.5 %. And Jing et al. [25] prepared CDs with a yield of 43.8 % by hydrothermal carbonization and oxidation with glucose as carbon source, NaOH and H2O2 as oxidant, which was far more than that of direct hydrothermal under the same conditions. Therefore, it is important to improve the yield of LCDs by promoting the reconversion of hydrothermal char by gentle means, and oxidative stripping can efficiently convert biochar into LCDs. Considering that the preparation of heteroatom-doped LCDs by one-pot method produce high yield of hydrothermal char, the combination of heteroatom doping and hydrothermal char oxidation might be an effective method to improve the yield and fluorescence characteristics.

Therefore, in this study, a two-step process of heteroatom doping via hydrothermal as well as hydrothermal char oxidation was proposed to prepare the LCDs with high yield and intense fluorescent. The highly fluorescent N and S co-doped LCDs and hydrothermal char were prepared by hydrothermal treatment with mild sulfanilic acid as additive, and then the hydrothermal char as carbon source was further oxidized by NaOH and H2O2 to prepare high-yield oxidized LCDs (O-LCDs). And the structure, optical properties and optical stability of N and S co-doped LCDs were characterized to reveal the effects of N and S doping, and the mechanism of synergistic influence of heteroatoms on the structure and function of LCDs were further explored, which provided guidance for the application of high-quality LCDs.

Alkaline lignin (AL, 96 %) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China), which was dried to constant weight in a drying oven at 105 °C, and then put into a drying dish for use. H2O2 (30 %, AR), and HCl (37 %, AR) were purchased from Hushi Laboratory Equipment Co. (Shanghai, China). NaOH (96 %, AR), sulfanilic acid (99.5 %, AR) and quinine sulfatewas (98.6 %, AR) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent (Shanghai, China). The microporous filtration membrane with diameter of 0.22 μm prepared by Tianjin Jinteng Company. The molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) of the dialysis bag was 3000 Da, which was prepared by Xi'an UB Biotechnology Co. Deionized water (DI) was used throughout this experiment.

N and S co-doped LCDs and oxidized LCDs were synthesized by a two-step hydrothermal oxidation process, as shown in Fig. 1 . Briefly, 0.25 g of alkali lignin was added to 50 mL of deionized water at room temperature with continuous stirring, until alkali lignin was completely dissolved. Meanwhile, 0.25g of sulfanilic acid was added into solution. Then, the mixed solution was transferred to a high-temperature and high-pressure reactor equipped with a para-polyphenol (PPL) liner for hydrothermal reaction. The reaction procedure was as follows: the reactor was heated from 20 °C to the required reaction temperature at 3 °C/min and maintained for 4 h, and the reaction process maintained a stirring rate of 400 r/min. After the reaction, the reactor was cooled to room temperature. The mixture was purified by 0.22 μm microporous filter membrane, and the hydrothermal carbon and the filtrate containing N and S doped LCDs were collected respectively. The hydrothermal carbon was weighed after drying in a 105 °C drying oven, and then was added to the PPL lining for hydrothermal reaction. Meanwhile, 35 mL deionized water, 12 mL H2O2 and 3 mL NaOH aqueous solution (1 mol/L) also were added to the lining. The hydrothermal reaction procedure was as follows: the temperature heated up to 150 °C from 20 °C at a heating rate of 3 °C/min, and retained for 90 min, the reaction process maintained a stirring rate of 400 r/min. After the reaction, the mixture was separated by a microporous filter membrane. And the filtrate containing oxidized LCDs was obtained.

The filtrates containing the two LCDs were transferred to dialysis bags, respectively, and then the dialysis bags were transferred to a beaker containing 1 L deionized water for dialysis purification. The deionized water was changed every 6 h and dialysis continued for 2 days to remove light molecular fluorescent substances. After the dialysis, the liquids in the dialysis bags were collected and freeze-dried at −60 °C to collect the two LCDs powders. N and S co-doped LCDs were named 160-NS-LCDs, 180-NS-LCDs, 200-NS-LCDs and 220-NS-LCDs according to different hydrothermal reaction temperatures. Oxidized LCDs were named 160-O-LCDs, 180-O-LCDs, 200-O-LCDs, and 220-O-LCDs. The detailed calculated methods of the fluorescence QY, Time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) spectra and average fluorescence lifetime have described in previous study [26]. The fluorescence QY of LCDs as the ratio of emitted photons to absorbed photons was determined by the relative method [27]. Briefly, quinine sulfate was dissolved in 0.1 mol L−1 H2SO4 as a fluorescence reference solution, and the reference fluorescence QY was 54 %. To minimize the inner filter effect, the reference solution and LCDs solution were appropriately diluted and their absorbance was adjusted to ∼0.05. The PL emission spectrum was measured at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. The calculation formula for the fluorescence QY of LCDs is as follows [28]:
(1)
φ s
=
φ R
I s
I R
A R
A s
(
η s
η R
)
2
where φ indicates the fluorescence QY. I indicate the integrated emission intensity, A indicates the optical density, and η indicates the refractive index (1.33 for aqueous solution). The subscript R indicates the reference sample of quinine sulfate, and the subscript S indicates the reference sample of LCDs samples.

Besides, the calculated method of the yields of two LCDs and solid products is as follows:
(1)
Y i e l d
(
wt . %
)
=
W S
W
A l
+
W O
× 100 %
where Yield indicates the yields of LCDs or solid products, W
s represents the mass of recovered LCDs or solid products, W
Al represents the mass of AL, and W
O represents the mass of organic acids.

The morphology of LCDs was characterized by a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM, Tecnai G2 F20, FEI, USA). The microcrystalline structure of the LCDs was tested by X-ray diffraction (XRD, x'pert3 powder, PANalytical, Netherlands). And Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Nicolet iS50R, Thermo Scientific, USA) and x-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, AXIS-ULTRA DLD-600W, Shimadzu-Kratos, Japan) were used to detect the functional group and chemical composition information of LCDs, respectively. The detailed information of methods for measuring performance has been described in previous publication [26].

Besides, The UV–vis absorption spectra of the LCDs and quinine sulfate solution were measured by a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV–vis, Lambda 35, PerkinElmer, USA). The test range was 200–800 nm, the accuracy was 0.08 nm. Excitation spectra, emission spectra, time-resolved photoluminescence spectra, and fluorescence stability tests of LCDs were measured by a steady-state transient modular fluorescence spectrometer (QuantaMaster 8000, HORIBA, Canada). The excitation light source was xenon lamp, and the signal was collected by 914D detector. At the incident side of the sample bin, 400 nm bandpass filter was used to remove the clutter signal at the excited end. The test method was 3D scanning mode. The Rayleigh scattering peak and frequency doubling peak of the sample were eliminated by Origin fitting.

The pH stability, temperature stability and dynamic stability of LCDs were tested by transient steady-state modular fluorescence spectrometer. Detailed operation is as follows: 1 mol/L HCl solution and 1 mol/L NaOH solution were used to prepare LCDs solution with pH ranging from 1 to 13, and the emission curve of LCDs solution at 354 nm excitation wavelength was measured by fluorescence spectrometer, and the pH stability of LCDs fluorescence was evaluated by integrating the emission curve. A heating module was used to control the temperature of LCDs solution, and the temperature was raised at 5 oC/min from 10 °C to the setting temperature. After temperature equilibrium, the emission curve of LCDs was tested. The emission curve of LCDs from 10 °C to 90 °C was recorded at 10 °C interval, and the temperature stability of LCDs fluorescence was characterized by integration. LCDs solution was continuously excited by a 75 W oval xenon lamp for 90 min, and the dynamic steady-state test of LCDs was carried out by a single photon detector to characterize its emission stability under high intensity continuous excitation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The yields of LCDs and solid residue
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 shows the yields of NS-LCDs, O-LCDs and solid residue at different temperature of hydrothermal process. The yield of solid residue increases from 14.76 wt% to 19.12 wt% with the temperature increasing from 160 °C to 220 °C, and the total yield of LCDs increases from 12.39 wt.% to 17.13 wt.% at 200 °C and then decreases to 15.51 wt.% at 220 °C, among which the yields of NS-LCDs and O-LCDs increases first and then decreases with the increase of temperature, reaching the maximum of 5.71 wt.% and 11.42 wt.% at 220 °C, respectively. Compared with previous study [26], the substitution of sulfanilic acid for HNO3 significantly decreased the yield of solid residue and increased the yield of NS-LCDs, which might be related to the mild acidity of sulfanilic acid. If the yield of O-LCDs is calculated on the basis of solid residue, the yield is stable at more than 50 %, which proves that oxidation stripping is an efficient method to synthesize LCDs. Therefore, the hydrothermal oxidation process increases the total yield of LCDs to 17.13 wt%, which has significant advantages for large-scale synthesis of LCDs. In order to expand its application prospect, it is still necessary to analyze its optical characteristics to evaluate its comprehensive performance.

The morphological characteristics of 180-NS-LCDs and 180-O-LCDs were detected by using HR-TEM, as shown in Fig. 3 . And 180-NS-LCDs and 180-O-LCDs appear homogeneous quasi-spherical nanoparticles in aqueous solution, and have good monodispersion. Besides, Nano Measurer was used to randomly select 100 sample points in each of the 180-NS-LCDs and 180-O-LCDs images to measure the mean particle size of LCDs and plot the particle size distribution, as show in Fig. 3 e and f. The average particle size of 180-NS-LCDs prepared by using sulfanilic acid as additive is 3.61 nm, while the average particle size of 180-O-LCDs obtained by hydrothermal char oxidation is increased to 3.92 nm. It is reported that the influence of oxidative stripping on the formation process of LCDs is relatively complex, so the obtained LCDs tend to have large particle sizes and uneven distribution [29]. Besides, according to HR-TEM, no obvious lattice is observed for both LCDs, which might be related to the short reaction time of LCDs preparation process and the absence of additives such as HNO3 to promote graphitization. The carbon skeleton and microcrystalline structures of NS-LCDs and O-LCDs are shown in Fig. S1. Both NS-LCDs and O-LCDs have a diffraction halo near 20–25°, which indicates that the LCDs have obvious amorphous structure, and the XRD pattern and peak position of the two LCDs do not change significantly with the increase of reaction temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect on chemical constitution of LCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The functional group information of NS-LCDs and O-LCDs prepared at different temperatures are shown in Fig. 4 . And Table S1 summarizes the absorbance details of the main values of NS-LCDs and O-LCDs. It is found that the surface functional group distribution of 160-NS-LCDs is similar to that of lignin, indicating that low temperature retains more of the primary functional groups of lignin. When the reaction temperature is higher than 180 °C, the peak type, peak position and number of infrared absorption peaks of NS-LCDs change significantly. With the increase of temperature, the peak strength of phenol hydroxyl group (O-H) of NS-LCDs decreases obviously, it might be due to that the increase of temperature causes a severe dehydration reaction of NS-LCDs. And the peak strength of methoxy C-H increases gradually, while the change trend of methylene is the same, which might be attributed to that the aromatic side chain is not seriously damaged with sulfanilic acid. The peaks of the aromatic ring skeleton vibration (1460–1605 cm−1) are the G-type lignin marker peak, which are retained in NS-LCDs when the temperature is lower than 180 °C, but which are significantly damaged with the temperature higher than 180 °C. And the peak at 829 cm−1 is the C-H of guaiac monomer, and the peak strength decreases gradually with the temperature rising, and almost disappears at 200 °C, which further confirms the damage of the primary aromatic structure in NS-LCDs at high temperature.

Besides, the peak of mercaptan structure (S-H, 2600 cm−1) increases gradually with the temperature increasing from 180 °C to 220 °C, which indicates that S begins to be doped on LCDs at 180 °C, and the amount of doping increases with the increase of temperature. And the absorption peak of C-S/C=S (600-800 cm−1) increases with temperature increasing, which proves that the temperature rising is conducive to the combination of S and LCDs defect sites to form doping structures [10,30]. Moreover, the absorption peak of C-O/C-N (1129–1180 cm−1) remains basically unchanged as the temperature increases, which might be due to that there are a large number of carboxyl, hydroxyl, amino and other hydrophilic functional groups to be formed in NS-LCDs [31]. Thus, it indicates that the doping effect of N and S is significant when the reaction temperature is higher than 180 °C, making NS-LCDs obtain more abundant surface structures.

FTIR spectra of O-LCDs are shown in Fig. 4b. The functional group structures of O-LCDs prepared from hydrothermal char obtained at different temperatures are basically the same. The peaks of aromatic skeleton vibration (594 cm−1) are always higher than those of NS-LCDs, which indicates that O-LCDs have abundant aromatic structures. And O-LCDs obtained from oxidation have more significant carboxyl absorption peak (1500 cm−1) than that of NS-LCDs, which might be due to that oxidation treatment leads to a large number of carboxyl structures to be formed on the surface of O-LCDs. Besides, the absorption peaks (600-1500 cm−1) significantly decrease or disappear, which might be attributed to that C-S, C-N doping structures in O-LCDs are destroyed by oxidation treatment, while some hydroxyls, carboxyl, carbonyl and other oxygen-containing functional groups in O-LCDs are retained due to the strong oxidation environment, which gives O-LCDs a strong water solubility.

To further verify the results of FT-IR spectroscopy, XPS spectral analysis was performed to detect the surface chemical composition of LCDs. The XPS survey spectra of NS-LCDs and O-LCDs are shown in Fig. 5 a and b. The peaks at 168.5 eV, 285.0 eV, 400.1 eV and 532.2 eV belong to S 2p, C 1s, N 1s and O 1s, respectively. The surface chemical compositions of NS-LCDs are mainly composed of C, O, N and S, which once again proves the doping of nitrogen and sulfur elements. O-LCDs is mainly composed of C and O elements, and also contains a little of N and S elements, which indicates oxidation treatment might destroy the nitrogen- and sulfur-doping structures. Besides, it is found a weak Auger peak of Na 1s at 497.9 eV, which might be a trace of NaOH residue. Fig. 5 also shows the high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s, O 1s, N 1s, and S 2p of LCDs. And Table S2 shows the relative content of C, O, N and S elements and the C-containing functional groups of LCDs based on XPS results. With the increase of reaction temperature, oxygen content in NS-LCDs increases from 30.4 % to 32.0 %, and O/C increases from 0.50 to 0.55, meanwhile the total doping amounts of N and S increase from 8.8 % to 9.5 %, respectively. Combined with FTIR spectroscopy, it proves again that NS-LCDs retains more AL structure at low reaction temperature. Thus, reaction temperature increasing is conducive to the destruction of the original structure of AL, and promotes the dopant of N and S elements to form more abundant N-, S- and oxygen-containing functional groups. Meanwhile, the N element in NS-LCDs mainly exists in the form of amine nitrogen (N-H) and graphite nitrogen (C-N), and the N element content increases slightly at higher temperature, which is mainly transformed into more stable graphite nitrogen (as shown in Fig. 5e), and the presence of graphite nitrogen is conducive to the redshift of emission wavelength in NS-LCDs. The S element in NS-LCDs mainly exists in the form of C-SO2, C-SO3 and C-SO4 (as shown in Fig. 5f), which indicates that sulfanilic acid forms a stable doping structure in LCDs. Besides, with the temperature increasing, the carbon content of C-C/C=C increases slightly from 35.4 % to 37.6 %, and the content of C-O-C/C-OH decreases from 22.3 % to 17.1 %, which might be due to that temperature increasing slightly promote the depolymerization of AL and the carbon skeleton growth of NS-LCDs sp2 [17,32].

The XPS peaking results of C1s, O 1s, N 1s, and S 2p of O-LCDs are shown in Table S2. Compared with NS-LCDs, O-LCDs has higher C content and lower N and S contents, the N and S contents reduce to about 1–2%, which might be due to the fracture of nitrogen and sulfur structures in the preparation of O-LCDs by oxidation process of hydrothermal char. Meanwhile, the C element increases from 69.2 % to 72.2 % with temperature rising to 200 °C, then decreases slightly with the temperature further increasing, which indicates that high temperature (220 °C) might destroy the carbon skeleton structure. Besides, the XPS peaking results of C1s is shown in Fig. 5g, it is found that O-LCDs have higher contents of C–O–C/C–OH and C=O in comparison with that of NS-LCDs, which is corresponded to the results of FTIR (as shown in Fig. 4). It indicates that the oxidation process promotes the formation of C-O-C/C-OH and C=O structures on the surface of LCDs due to its strong oxidation environment. The results of FT-IR and XPS proves that sulfanilic acid as dopant participates in the formation of LCDs, and results in NS-LCDs a highly functional surface. To further study the effects of N and S co-doping on the fluorescence characteristics of LCDs, the optical properties of the two LCDs need to further analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect on optical properties of LCDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 6 shows the UV–vis spectrum of NS-LCDs solution and its photographs in daylight/violet light. All NS-LCDs solutions are clear light-yellow solutions in sunlight, and with the increase of reaction temperature, the fluorescence of NS-LCDs gradually changes from green to cyan and then to blue under 365 nm UV lamp, which indicates that the emission wavelength of NS-LCDs changes blue. Besides, the four NS-LCDs show similar absorption peaks in the wavelength range of 200–300 nm in the UV–vis spectrum, which might be attributed to the π-π∗ transition of C=C in the carbon core. And the absorption peak intensity gradually increases with the rise of reaction temperature, which indicates that the intrinsic state luminescence of the carbon core increases gradually. Meanwhile, four NS-LCDs show weak absorption peaks in the wavelength range of 300–400 nm, which might be attributed to the n-π∗ transition caused by the surface functional group (C-O/C-N/C-S) of NS-LCDs. Moreover, all O-LCDs solutions are also clear light-yellow solutions in daylight, and their fluorescence gradually changes from cyan to green under 365 nm UV lamps. Moreover, there is no obvious absorption peak of O-LCDs to be observed, and the absorption peaks of O-LCDs fall to 0 at about 450–500 nm, while the absorption edges of NS-LCDs are 400–450 nm, which indicates that the light absorption of O-LCDs in the visible region is conducive to the long-wave emission of LCDs.

Fig. 7 shows the 3D PL spectra of NS-LCDs and O-LCDs. And the excitation and emission centers as well as fluorescence are summarized in Table 1 . With the increase of excitation wavelength, the emission peak wavelength of 160-NS-LCDs and all O-LCDs have different degrees of redshift, which might be due to that 160-NS-LCDs prepared at lower temperature has more surface defects. And with the increase of reaction temperature, the surface order of NS-LCDs prepared also improves and the defect sites decrease. However, the oxidation process might destroy the structure of hydrothermal char, resulting in the increase of surface defects in O-LCDs, and the formation of more defect state-related energy levels. And when excitation waves of different wavelengths are excited successively, different fluorescence radiation are produced, and then the phenomenon of excitation dependence is shown. 180-NS-LCDs, 200-NS-LCDs and 220-NS-LCDs might have fewer surface defects, which exhibit strong excitation independence [33]. And the formation of C-SOX structure also increases the excitation independence of LCDs [34].

Moreover, with the increase of reaction temperature, the optimal excitation wavelength of NS-LCDs shifts from 398 nm to 354 nm, and the optimal emission wavelength shifts from 503 nm to 473 nm, which might be due to that the heteroatom-doping effect of NS-LCDs significantly affects the blue shift according to XRD, FT-IR and XPS analysis, and the electron donor structures such as pyridine and pyrrole might induce blue shift emission behavior. In contrast to NS-LCDs, the optimal excitation wavelength of O-LCDs is redshifted from 398 nm to 450 nm, and the optimal emission wavelength is redshifted from 515 nm to 551 nm with the reaction temperature increasing. It might be due to the increase of sp2 carbon nuclei and the abundant electron-absorbing carboxyl group structure of O-LCDs surface.

On the other hand, the fluorescence QY of NS-LCDs firstly increases and then decreases with the increase of reaction temperature, and reaches the maximum value of 18.00 % at 200-NS-LCDs, which is increased by ∼7.7 times in comparison with 160-NS-LCDs (as shown in Table 1). It is found that the variation trend of fluorescence QY of NS-LCDs is the same as the variation trend of C-C/C=C and C-N content, which might be attributed to that the fluorescence of NS-LCDs is affected by both carbon core eigenstates and surface functional groups. With the increase of temperature, NS-LCDs obtain more complete sp2 carbon nuclei. Meanwhile, the doping effect of N and S is more significant at higher temperature, and more stable C-N and C-S structures are formed in NS-LCDs, which might change the electron cloud density and reduce the non-radiative recombination, and promote the improvement of fluorescence QY. Compared with NS-LCDs, the fluorescence QY of O-LCDs is lower, which might be related to the significant reduction of doping structure in O-LCDs. In addition, the surface of O-LCDs is rich in carboxyl groups, which might absorb some radiation as an electron-absorbing structure, and result in non-radiative recombination of local electron-hole pairs of O-LCDs, thereby which further reduce fluorescence QY.

The TRPL spectra of the two LCDs were further analyzed to analyze their fluorescence characteristics. As shown in Fig. 8 , when the temperature is higher than 160 °C, the fluorescence attenuation curve of NS-LCDs changes significantly, and the fluorescence lifetime of NS-LCDs is significantly extended above 180 °C. It might be related to the LCDs surface structure, and 160-NS-LCDs has the least carbon skeleton (C-C/C=C) structure. Besides, compared with NS-LCDs, the fluorescence decay curve of O-LCDs decays significantly earlier. Considering that O-LCDs has fewer N and S doping, thus the influence of impurity doping on the fluorescence lifetime of LCDs might be crucial. Moreover, the fluorescence decay curves of O-LCDs are basically the same, which indicates that the PL process of all O-LCDs is similar.

The fluorescence attenuation curve of NS-LCDs is fitted by a double exponential function. The fitting curve consists of short-lived τ1 and long-lived component τ2, corresponding to the recombination process of eigenstates π -- π∗ and surface states n -- π∗, respectively. The proportions of the two components are A1 and A2. The fitting parameters of fluorescence attenuation curves of NS-LCDs and O-LCDs are summarized in Table 2 . It is found that the fluorescence lifetime of NS-LCDs increases from 10.55ns to 27.80ns with the temperature increasing from 160 °C to 220 °C, and the proportion of A2 increases from 16.52 % to 62.03 %. It indicates that the fluorescence lifetime of NS-LCDs prepared at 160 °C is mainly affected by carbon core eigenstates. And with the increase of temperature, N and S doping enhance, and the surface states related to functional groups gradually dominate fluorescence, showing that the proportion of long-lived components gradually increases, which significantly extends the average fluorescence lifetime of NS-LCDs. For O-LCDs, the N and S functional groups are greatly reduced, and the surface state fluorescence is limited. The short-life component of the four O-LCDs is all ∼80 %, indicating that their fluorescence lifetime is almost completely determined by the core state, so O-LCDs exhibit a short fluorescence lifetime.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Regulation mechanism of NS-LCDs and O-LCDs
ce:section.ce:para: LCDs prepared by lignin thermochemical transformation have complex and diverse structures, and fluorescence emission mechanism of which is mainly divided into carbon core state and surface state. Based on this, the optical regulatory mechanism of NS-LCDs and O-LCDs is analyzed, and the structures and fluorescence characteristics of the two LCDs are summarized in Fig. 9 . For 160-NS-LCDs and O-LCDs, the fluorescence color is mainly blue-green, which is mainly due to that the fluorescence emission is dominated by the carbon core state, and the π-π ∗ transition of sp2 domain in the carbon core is the main source of the blue fluorescence emission of LCDs. Besides, the surface of 160-NS-LCDs and O-LCDs have a large number of defect structures due to low hydrothermal temperature or oxidation process, which might cause the LCDs to produce multiple energy levels. Thus, photons recombine in various ways when LCDs excite by different excitation wavelengths, resulting 160-NS-LCDs and O-LCDs show strong excitation dependence. In addition, according to the results of XPS, 160-NS-LCDs and O-LCDs also contain abundant electron absorbing structures such as amino group and carboxyl group, which might cause the non-radiative recombination of electron-hole pairs and reduce the fluorescence QY.

The fluorescence emission centers of 180-NS-LCDs, 200-NS-LCDs, and 220-NS-LCDs are similar and exhibit excitation independence, which might be mainly attributed to the decrease of unstable defect structure on the surface of LCDs with the increase of hydrothermal temperature. With the formation of C-N and C-S structures, the fluorescence of LCDs is dominated by surface state emission. Moreover, the variation trend of fluorescence QY and C-N contents of the three LCDs are consistent, which indicates that the formation of stable N-containing structure might be the key to improve the fluorescence QY of LCDs. And the presence of these S-containing functional groups, mainly existing in the form of C-SOx, might improve the fluorescence stability of LCDs and reduce excitation dependence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of fluorescence stability of NS-LCDs
ce:section.ce:para: 200-NS-LCDs was chosen to further explore the stability of pH, temperature and continuous excitation. The PL spectra of 200-NS-LCDs at different pH values were measured at a fixed excitation wavelength of 354 nm to evaluate the pH stability. As shown in Fig. 10 a, when the pH of 200-NS-LCDs solution decreases from 13 to 1, its emission peak shifts from ∼435 nm to ∼480 nm. According to Fig. 10b, the emission intensity of 200-NS-LCDs solution is higher under weak acidity (pH = 2–5), while its emission intensity is significantly reduced under strong acidity, neutral and alkaline conditions. The variation of emission intensity and wavelength with pH might be related to the protonation and deprotonation of functional groups on the surface of 200-NS-LCDs. It has been reported that sulfonic acid groups are prone to deprotonation in acidic environments, so 200-NS-LCDs exhibit high radiation recombination efficiency at pH = 2–5 [17]. However, the pH continues to decrease, and the fluorescence intensity of 200-NS-LCDs declines sharply, which might be related to saturated protonation under strongly acidic conditions [35].

The PL spectra of 200-NS-LCDs at different temperatures were measured to evaluate their pH stability with a fixed excitation wavelength of 354 nm. As shown in Fig. 10c, as the solution temperature increases from 10 °C to 90 °C, the emission peak position of 200-NS-LCDs remains at 480 nm, and the peak intensity decreases linearly. Besides, Fig. 10d shows the relative fluorescence intensity of 200-NS-LCDs, which is calculated by the integration of fluorescence intensity at different temperatures. The relative fluorescence intensity of 200-NS-LCDs is linearly fitted using Origin software. The linear regression formula is indicated in Fig. 10d, and the correlation coefficient (R2) is 0.996. Based on this, the fluorescence of 200-NS-LCDs shows an obvious linear relationship with temperature, which is conducive to expanding its application in the field of environmental detection.

Moreover, the light stability of 200-NS-LCDs under continuous excitation was tested with a fixed excitation wavelength of 354 nm to verify its practicability. As shown in Fig. 11 , the relative PL strength of 200-NS-LCDs remains above 95 % under continuous excitation of 75 W oval xenon lamp for 90 min. The decrease of PL strength might be attributed to the heating of 200-NS-LCDs solution under continuous excitation, thus 200-NS-LCDs shows excellent photobleaching resistance.

In this study, NS-LCDs and O-LCDs with high yield were prepared by the two-step process of hydrothermal and oxidation. By characterizing the structure and optical properties of NS-LCDs and O-LCDs, the effects of N and S doping and oxidation on the structure and optical properties of LCDs were revealed. It was found that the yields of NS-LCDs initially increased and then decreased with the increase of reaction temperature, reaching a maximum value of 5.71 wt.% at 200 °C. And more than 50 % of the hydrothermal char was converted to O-LCDs by oxidative stripping, which resulted in the total yield of LCDs was further increased to 17.13 wt.% at 200 °C. The surfaces of 160-NS-LCDs and O-LCDs had a large number of defect structures, which made multiple energy levels and showed strong excitation dependence, and the unstable defect structures of LCDs were greatly reduced when the reaction temperature was higher than 180 °C, showing excitation independence. Meanwhile, the fluorescence QY of NS-LCDs showed a similar trend with mass yield, obtained a maximum value of 18.00 % at 200 °C, and C-N might significantly improve the fluorescence QY of LCDs, C-SOx might improve the fluorescence stability of LCDs and reduce excitation dependence. Besides, 200-NS-LCDs had good acid stability, temperature stability and photobleaching resistance.

Hanping Chen: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Jiawen Zeng: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis. Zhiqiang Chen: Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Mingwei Xia: Validation, Methodology, Investigation. Ying Gao: Resources, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Danchen Zhu: Validation, Methodology, Investigation. Ziyue Tang: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Investigation, Funding acquisition. Haiping Yang: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Yingquan Chen: Validation, Conceptualization. Xianhua Wang: Validation, Supervision, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There is no conflict to be declared.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors wish to express their great appreciation for the financial support from the National Nature Science Foundation of China (52125601; 52306244; 52476202). And the technical support from the Analytical and Testing Center at Huazhong University of Science & Technology (http://atc.hust.edu.cn).
